mnemonics.title: Insertion Order
mnemonics.content: I.N.S.E.R.T: Identify position → NULL the new node's next → Set previous node's next to new → Extract old next to new's next → Return success → Traverse to verify (optional)

mnemonics.title: Deletion Order
mnemonics.content: D.E.L.E.T.E: Display/Find node → Extract next pointer → Link previous to next → Update head/tail if needed → Execute free() on removed node

mnemonics.title: Traversal Steps
mnemonics.content: T.R.A.V.E.R.S.E: Temp = head → Repeat while Temp ≠ NULL → Visit (print/process) → Update Temp = Temp->next → End loop → Return result

acronyms.usage: Used to represent a linear data structure with nodes linked by pointers
First node pointer that provides entry point to entire list

acronyms.expansion: No Useful Link or No Value
acronyms.usage: Marker indicating end of linked list; prevents infinite traversal

visualTips.visualization: Draw boxes: [DATA | NEXT→] where DATA is a single box and NEXT is an arrow pointing to another node box or NULL (⊥)
Two-step: 1) New node's arrow points to existing head 2) Head arrow redirects to new node - like adding a new first car to a train
Show three nodes A→B→C, when deleting B: A's arrow now points directly to C, B becomes disconnected then fades away
Use a 'walker' icon moving along arrows from HEAD through each node, stopping at NULL like a wall

analogies.analogy: Like a treasure hunt where each clue (node) points to the next clue. You must start at the first clue (head) and follow pointers in order.
Each node is like a train car with passengers (data) and a coupling (next pointer) connecting to the next car.

analogies.analization: NULL is like the platform at the end of a train station - the last car couples to the platform, not another car.

analogies.analogy: Unlike arrays (hotel rooms must be contiguous), linked lists are like a scavenger hunt that can continue anywhere - no room booking needed.
